Algeria.text: Algeria squeezed through the group stage on the strength of a nerve-shredding 3-3 draw with Austria — a match that swung violently in both directions, Mahrez equalising deep into stoppage time only for Kalajdzic to restore parity three minutes later, leaving the Fennec Foxes advancing as one of the better third-placed sides rather than outright qualifiers. They meet Switzerland in the Round of 32 on Thu Jul 2 20:00 PT, a genuinely open contest: the Swiss are organised and difficult to break down, but Algeria's attacking instincts, demonstrated in that six-goal thriller, make this no foregone conclusion either way. Survive that, and Colombia or Ghana await in the Round of 16, before a potential quarterfinal collision with Argentina — a fixture that would represent a true reckoning with Algeria's continental ambitions.

Argentina.text: Argentina swept through Group J without dropping a point, and the 3-1 defeat of Jordan was a composed, professional exercise — two first-half goals put the game to bed before a Jordan consolation briefly complicated the scoreline, and Messi's 80th-minute finish was the champions' way of closing the door with authority. Cape Verde arrive in the Round of 32 on Fri Jul 3 15:00 PT as heavy underdogs; this is a routine assignment on paper, though the African side's physical directness means Argentina must impose their tempo early or risk an uncomfortable afternoon. Survive that, and Colombia or Switzerland could wait in the quarterfinal — either tie carrying genuine weight before what might be a defining semifinal confrontation with Brazil, England, or Norway.

Australia.text: A goalless draw against Paraguay to close the group stage — functional rather than inspiring, Australia doing enough to bank second place without ever threatening to illuminate proceedings. They qualified, and in knockout football that is the only arithmetic that matters. Egypt await in the Round of 32 on Fri Jul 3 11:00 PT, a winnable tie on paper but one that demands more attacking conviction than Australia have shown; the Pharaohs are no soft touch, and Australia will need to rediscover an edge they have kept largely sheathed. Survive that, and Argentina could be the Round of 16 obstacle — a reminder of how steep the mountain becomes.

Austria.text: Austria scraped through Group J on the back of a breathless 3-3 draw with Algeria — Kalajdzic's equaliser deep into stoppage time rescuing a point after Mahrez had twice put Algeria ahead and the match had swung violently in both directions. It was the kind of result that keeps a side in the tournament but raises as many questions as it answers. Spain await in the Round of 32 on Thu Jul 2 12:00 PT, a formidable assignment by any measure: Austria are the clear underdogs against one of Europe's perennial heavyweights, and their defensive frailties will need urgent attention. Survive that, and Croatia or Portugal could follow in the Round of 16 — a bracket offering no mercy whatsoever.

Belgium.text: Belgium closed out the group stage in emphatic fashion, dismantling New Zealand 5-1 in a performance that never looked in doubt — Trossard's brace set the tone before half-time, and Bruyne and Lukaku added gloss in a second half that turned into a procession. They face Senegal in the Round of 32 on Wed Jul 1 13:00 PT, a tie Belgium enter as clear favourites, though Senegal are no pushover and will fancy their athleticism and organisation to make this competitive. A Round of 16 date with either Bosnia & Herzegovina or the USA awaits the winner, before the draw opens onto a potential quarterfinal against Portugal or Spain — the kind of test that will reveal whether this generation can finally deliver on a decade of expectation.

Bosnia & Herzegovina.text: Bosnia & Herzegovina closed the group stage with conviction, putting Qatar away 3-1 in a performance that never felt in doubt — two goals before the half-hour mark settled the contest, and Mahmic's late finish was confirmation rather than consolation. They advance as a best third-placed team, which sets up an immediate test of the sternest kind: the host nation USA in the Round of 32 on Wed Jul 1 17:00 PT, a fixture where Bosnia enter as considerable underdogs against a side playing on home soil with real momentum behind them. Survive that, and Belgium or Senegal await in the Round of 16, with the possibility of Portugal or Spain lurking in the quarterfinals.

Brazil.text: Brazil dispatched Scotland with something close to contempt — two goals from Júnior either side of half-time, the first arriving inside seven minutes, effectively ended the contest before Cunha added a third after the hour. Three wins from three, a seven-goal swing in goal difference, and the Seleção are moving through the group stage with the kind of unhurried authority that tends to unsettle knockout opponents. Japan await in the Round of 32 on Mon Jun 29 10:00 PT, a well-organised side capable of disruption but not quite the calibre to trouble a Brazil in this mood. Should they advance, either Ivory Coast or Norway stand in the way in the Round of 16, before the draw opens toward a potential quarterfinal collision with England or Mexico — the sort of fixture that turns a tournament.

Canada.text: Canada survive their Round of 32 on the slenderest of margins — Stephen Eustáquio's goal deep in stoppage time the difference against South Africa in a contest that offered precious little comfort until that very last moment. They advance, but the manner of it raises questions a co-host cannot afford to leave unanswered. Saturday's Round of 16 (Sat Jul 4 10:00 PT) presents a stiff examination: Morocco or the Netherlands await, and either represents a considerable step up — this is no routine assignment for a side that barely edged past South Africa. Should Canada find a way through, a quarterfinal on Thu Jul 9 could bring France or Germany into view, the kind of opposition that would define a generation for Canadian football.

Cape Verde.text: Cape Verde ground out three draws across the group stage without conceding a goal, the goalless stalemate with Saudi Arabia the epitome of their tournament so far — disciplined, compact, and short on attacking adventure. That defensive solidity earns them a knockout place, but it also sets up one of the most forbidding Round of 32 assignments imaginable: Argentina, Fri Jul 3 15:00 PT, a contest in which the Blue Sharks are heavy underdogs against the world champions. Should the upset materialise, Australia or Egypt would await in the Round of 16, with Algeria, Colombia, Ghana, or Switzerland potentially looming in the quarterfinal.

Colombia.text: Colombia closed the group stage with a disciplined 0-0 against Portugal — a clean sheet against that calibre of opponent reads as a statement of defensive intent rather than a failure of nerve, and seven points from Group K confirms they finished in command. Ghana await in the Round of 32 on Fri Jul 3 18:30 PT, a fixture Colombia enter as clear favourites; the Black Stars are capable of disruption but this is Colombia's tie to control. Survive that, and a probable Round of 16 meeting with Algeria or Switzerland beckons, before a potential quarterfinal collision with Argentina that would define their tournament entirely.

Croatia.text: Croatia edged past Ghana in a group-stage finish that required a late hand — Vlasic's 83rd-minute goal restoring the lead after Ghana had levelled in the 73rd, turning what looked a comfortable afternoon into something more fraught. They qualified, but the performance carried a cautionary note. Portugal await in the Round of 32 on Thu Jul 2 16:00 PT, a formidable assignment by any measure: Croatia are capable of hurting anyone on the counter, yet this shapes as a genuine test against one of the tournament's heavyweight favourites. Survive that, and Spain or France loom as possible obstacles further down the draw.

Curaçao.text: Curaçao closed their group stage campaign with a 0-2 defeat to Ivory Coast, Nicolas Pépé striking early and then again just past the hour to make it a controlled, comfortable evening for the Elephants. The result confirmed what the tournament had largely borne out across three matches: Curaçao, plucky debutants on the greatest stage, were outgunned at this level. They exit with a single point, a goal difference of minus nine, and the distinction of having competed — if not quite threatened — against top-tier opposition in Group E.

Czech Republic.text: Czech Republic's group stage exit arrived with little ceremony — a 0-3 defeat to Mexico that was effectively settled inside the opening quarter-hour of the second half, Chávez and Quiñones striking within six minutes of each other before Fidalgo's stoppage-time finish applied a flattering coat of finality to what had already been a comfortable Mexican evening. The Czechs finished fourth in Group A with a single point and a goals difference of minus four, scraping through as one of the better third-placed sides before their tournament ended here, emphatically, on the wrong side of a mismatch they could never quite solve.

DR Congo.text: DR Congo finished their group stage with a statement of sorts — trailing to Shomurodov's early strike against Uzbekistan, they found their rhythm and ran out 3-1 winners, Wissa converting a penalty and then adding a second deep in stoppage time to bookend a Mayele goal that had already settled the nerves. The reward for advancing as a best third-placed side is an assignment that pulls few punches: England await in the Round of 32 on Wed Jul 1 09:00 PT, and by any reasonable assessment the Leopards enter as considerable underdogs against a side carrying genuine tournament expectations. Survive that, and Ecuador or Mexico loom in the Round of 16, before the quarterfinal bracket opens the possibility of Brazil or Ivory Coast — a run that would demand DR Congo produce something historically remarkable.

Ecuador.text: Germany took the lead inside two minutes, but Ecuador absorbed the early shock and methodically dismantled one of the tournament's heavyweight favourites — Angulo equalising before the quarter-hour and Plata sealing it with a composed finish thirteen minutes from time to send La Tri through as one of the best third-placed sides. A North American derby awaits in the Round of 32 against Mexico on Tue Jun 30 18:00 PT, a fixture that carries its own fierce continental rivalry; Ecuador arrive as slight underdogs against a Mexico side playing on home-adjacent soil with raucous support, making this anything but a straightforward assignment. Should they advance, England or DR Congo loom in the Round of 16, with Brazil or Norway possible opponents at the quarterfinal stage.

Egypt.text: Egypt finished their group campaign in unsatisfying fashion — Saber's fifth-minute opener against Iran was swiftly cancelled out by Rezaeian nine minutes later, and the Pharaohs had to settle for a draw that flattered neither side. They advance as group runners-up nonetheless, and face Australia in the Round of 32 on Fri Jul 3 11:00 PT — a winnable tie, though the Socceroos are a well-organised, combative side who will not capitulate quietly. Should Egypt progress, Argentina or Cape Verde await in the Round of 16, and a quarterfinal path could bring Colombia or Algeria into view.

England.text: England closed the group stage with a controlled, professional win over Panama — both goals arriving in a five-minute burst either side of the hour mark from Bellingham and Kane, a performance that was never spectacular but never needed to be. They top Group L and meet DR Congo in the Round of 32 on Wed Jul 1 09:00 PT, a tie England enter as firm favourites; Congo will carry ambition but England possess too much quality at this stage for it to be genuinely precarious. Survive that, and the draw opens enticingly — Brazil lurk as a potential quarterfinal opponent, with Argentina possible further along should England reach the semifinal.

France.text: Ousmane Dembélé dismantled Norway almost single-handedly, completing a hat-trick inside 32 minutes before Doué added gloss deep in stoppage time — a 4-1 hammering that doubles as a statement of intent from Group I winners. Sweden await in the Round of 32 on Tue Jun 30 14:00 PT, a fixture France approach as heavy favorites; the Swedes are a tidy, organized side, but Didier Deschamps's squad is operating at a different level entirely. Should they advance, Germany lurk as a potential Round of 16 collision, with Canada, Morocco, or the Netherlands possible obstacles in the quarterfinal beyond that.

Germany.text: Germany close out the group stage with a 1-2 defeat to Ecuador — Sané's second-minute opener gave way to Angulo's swift equaliser and a Plata strike on 77 minutes that left the Germans ragged at the back, though they advance as Group E winners regardless. They meet Paraguay in the Round of 32 on Mon Jun 29 13:30 PT, a fixture German sides of recent generations would expect to control, and nothing in Paraguay's standing suggests this edition should be different — yet the defensive frailties on display against Ecuador invite caution. Survive that, and France or Sweden await in the last 16, with the prospect of the Netherlands, Morocco, or Canada looming in the quarterfinal should Germany find their stride.

Ghana.text: Ghana squeezed through the group stage in third place after a frustrating defeat to Croatia — level at 1-1 going into the final ten minutes, they were undone by Vlasic's late winner to leave the door ajar rather than slam it shut. Colombia await in the Round of 32 on Fri Jul 3 18:30 PT, and that is a stiff assignment: a South American side with considerably more pedigree at this stage of a major tournament, making Ghana the underdog who will need to produce something well above their group-stage showing. Survive that, and either Algeria or Switzerland could follow in the Round of 16, before a potential quarterfinal confrontation with Argentina lurking as the most formidable name in that bracket.

Haiti.text: Haiti's final group game against Morocco ended 4-2, a scoreline that flatters neither side in quite the right way — the Haitians led briefly, equalized before half-time through Isidor, yet conceded four times across a match Morocco ultimately controlled with growing authority. Two late Moroccan goals in the final stages confirmed what the second half had suggested: Haiti were outclassed and outrun. They finish bottom of Group C without a point, their tournament defined by an 8-2 aggregate against them across three matches, and exit a World Cup that exposed the gulf between their ambition and the level required.

Iran.text: Iran's group stage ends with a 1-1 draw against Egypt — a match in which they surrendered the initiative inside five minutes before Rezaeian levelled early in the second quarter, and neither side could force a winner. Three draws across three games told the full story of a tournament in which Iran were never beaten but never convincing, accumulating just enough to squeeze through as one of the best third-placed sides. They exit at the round of 32, undone not by any single defeat but by a persistent inability to turn resistance into results.

Iraq.text: Iraq's group stage ended in humiliation against Senegal, a 5-0 thrashing that was effectively settled inside the first minute and ruthlessly extended across both halves. The Africans scored through five different players, the goals arriving in waves rather than any sustained Iraqi resistance. It was the punctuation mark on a tournament Iraq would sooner forget — three defeats, twelve goals conceded, none scored, and elimination from a competition they technically advanced into as a best third-placed side, only to find the step up in class absolute and unforgiving.

Ivory Coast.text: Nicolas Pépé settled Ivory Coast's group-stage nerves with a composed early goal and a second-half clincher against Curaçao — a controlled, professional finish that sent the Elephants through in second place without ever shifting out of a measured gear. They face Norway in the Round of 32 on Tue Jun 30 10:00 PT, a genuinely open contest: Norway arrive with Haaland's generation at full strength, but Ivory Coast's pedigree and African Cup experience make this a real coin-flip rather than a foregone conclusion. Survive that, and Brazil or Japan await in the Round of 16 — with a potential quarterfinal against England or Mexico looming as the tournament's true crucible for this side.

Japan.text: Japan edged through Group F in workmanlike fashion, leading Sweden before Elanga's equaliser six minutes later denied them a winning finish — a creditable result against decent opposition, if not quite the statement they might have wanted. Now the draw offers no mercy: Brazil await in the Round of 32 on Mon Jun 29 10:00 PT, a fixture that asks Japan to produce the performance of their tournament against one of the sport's imperial powers, making this a genuine test of whether Hajime Moriyasu's side have the defensive discipline and counter-punching quality to cause an upset. Survive that, and Ivory Coast or Norway could follow in the Round of 16, before the quarterfinal bracket opens the possibility of England or Mexico standing in the way of a semifinal.

Jordan.text:
Jordan's final group-stage outing against Argentina followed a familiar script: two goals down by the half-hour mark, Al-Tamari pulling one back just after the interval to briefly threaten a comeback, before Messi settled the contest with ten minutes to go. It was a respectable showing in impossible company, but the gulf in class was never seriously in doubt.

Winless across three matches and with a goal difference of minus five, Jordan nonetheless advanced as one of the best third-placed sides — only to exit at that stage, their tournament concluding without a knockout fixture to call their own.

Mexico.text: Mexico swept through Group A without conceding a goal, and their 3-0 dismissal of Czech Republic — two goals in a six-minute burst either side of the hour, then a fourth-official flourish deep in stoppage time — had the look of a side coasting well within themselves. They face Ecuador in the Round of 32 on Tue Jun 30 18:00 PT, a continental derby that carries genuine menace: Ecuador are no pushover, and CONMEBOL pride makes this a combustible coin-flip rather than a comfortable passage. Survive that, and England or DR Congo await in the Round of 16 — with Brazil a looming possibility in the quarterfinal beyond.

Morocco.text: Morocco finished their group stage with a flourish, recovering from an early scare against Haiti — two goals down by their own goalkeeper's misfortune before the half-hour mark — to run out 4-2 winners, with four unanswered goals confirming the superior quality. The Atlas Lions now face the Netherlands in the Round of 32 on Mon Jun 29 18:00 PT, a genuine examination of their ambitions: the Dutch arrive as clear favourites, but Morocco have repeatedly proved they can suffocate elite European sides and possess the individual quality to punish any complacency. Survive that, and Canada await in the Round of 16 before the draw potentially delivers France or Germany at the quarterfinal stage — the point at which Morocco's tournament would truly be written into history.

Netherlands.text: Netherlands finished their group campaign against Tunisia with the air of a side shifting through gears rather than straining them — Brian Brobbey struck inside seven minutes to cancel out an early Skhiri opener, and Jan Paul van Hecke's second-half finish settled a match the Dutch controlled without ever truly being threatened. Morocco await in the Round of 32 on Mon Jun 29 18:00 PT, a genuinely awkward proposition: the Atlas Lions are no soft touch, but a Dutch side that topped Group F with ten goals scored should carry reasonable favourites' expectations into the tie. Win that, and Canada stand in the way in the Round of 16, before a potential quarterfinal collision with France or Germany that would announce this tournament as something worth remembering.

New Zealand.text: Belgium dismantled New Zealand with something close to contempt, Trossard scoring either side of half-time before De Bruyne and Lukaku twisted the knife — the All Whites' consolation on 84 minutes a cosmetic gesture against a 5-1 final score. New Zealand's tournament is over: they finish fourth in Group G, their single point earned somewhere earlier in the group stage, the gap in class against Europe's elite ultimately impossible to bridge.

Norway.text: France dismantled Norway with ruthless efficiency in their final group game, Ousmane Dembélé completing a first-half hat-trick before a late fourth rubbed salt in the wound — Aasgaard's early equaliser a brief flicker of resistance in an otherwise one-sided afternoon. Norway qualified regardless, though the 4-1 scoreline is a sobering reminder of the distance between them and the tournament's elite. Ivory Coast await in the Round of 32 on Tue Jun 30 10:00 PT, a winnable tie on paper but one that will demand a sharper collective performance than this bruising send-off suggested. Survive that, and Brazil or Japan could loom in the Round of 16 — a considerably sterner examination of whether Norway's group-stage wins reflected genuine quality or favorable scheduling.

Panama.text: Panama's group stage exit was confirmed with a 0-2 defeat to England, a result that flattered neither side in the opening hour before Bellingham and Kane struck in quick succession midway through the second half to put the tie beyond doubt. Three losses, no points, and a four-goal deficit across Group L tells the full story of a tournament in which Panama never found their footing at this level. They depart having shown that the step up to a 48-team World Cup brought them no easier path to the knockout rounds.

Paraguay.text: A goalless draw against Australia secured Paraguay's passage through the group stage by the narrowest of margins — third place, four points, and a negative goal difference that tells its own story of a side that has ground out results rather than dazzled. They advance, but not with the wind at their backs. Germany await in the Round of 32 on Mon Jun 29 13:30 PT, and this is about as stern a first knockout assignment as the draw could have produced — a heavy favourite against a team that did not score in its final group game. Should Paraguay somehow find a way through, France or Sweden lurk in the Round of 16, with the Netherlands or Morocco possible obstacles in the quarterfinal beyond that.

Portugal.text: Portugal ground out a goalless draw with Colombia to close the group stage — a flat, functional performance that secured second place without ever threatening to ignite. They meet Croatia in the Round of 32 on Thu Jul 2 16:00 PT, a fixture that carries genuine weight: these are two sides who know each other well at major tournaments, and Croatia's tournament pedigree makes this a real contest rather than a formality, though Portugal's superior firepower should give them the edge. Survive that, and Spain or Austria could await in the Round of 16, with France or Germany lurking as possible semifinal opponents.

Qatar.text: Qatar's group stage exit arrived with little ceremony, a 1-3 defeat to Bosnia & Herzegovina that laid bare the limitations of a side that rarely threatened to trouble the tournament's upper tier. Behind by two goals before half-time following strikes from Alajbegovic and Abunada, Al-Haydos pulled one back on the stroke of the interval to offer a fleeting flicker of hope, only for Mahmic to extinguish it in the 80th minute. The hosts of 2022 finished bottom of Group B and depart with a single point to their name, a tournament record that reflects, honestly enough, where this squad currently stands among the world's best.

Saudi Arabia.text: Saudi Arabia's group stage ended with a goalless draw against Cape Verde that said little for either side — a flat, scoreless contest that confirmed the Green Falcons' limitations rather than dispelling them. Across three matches they managed a single goal, earned two draws, and finished fourth in Group H, advancing only by the grace of the best third-placed team standings. Their tournament ended there: not in a blaze of controversy or misfortune, but in the quiet arithmetic of a side that rarely threatened and ultimately ran out of road.

Scotland.text: Brazil ended Scotland's tournament with something close to contempt — Júnior struck twice before half-time, the second a stoppage-time flourish that summarised the gulf in class, and Cunha added a third after the interval to make it a thorough, joyless afternoon. Scotland had squeezed through as one of the better third-placed sides, a minor achievement rendered hollow by the manner of their exit. They leave the 2026 World Cup having won once in three matches, outscored four goals to one, and ultimately exposed by the first genuine elite opposition they faced.

Senegal.text: Senegal closed the group stage with a statement: a 5-0 demolition of Iraq that was effectively settled inside the first minute and then ruthlessly extended across the second half, Gueye netting twice to underline the margin. That result was enough to squeeze through as one of the best third-placed sides, though the reward is a daunting Round of 32 assignment against Belgium on Wed Jul 1 13:00 PT — a seasoned European side that will start as clear favourites and represents a genuine examination of Senegal's knockout credentials. Survive that, and Bosnia & Herzegovina or the United States await in the Round of 16, before a quarterfinal draw that could throw up Portugal or Spain.

South Africa.text: A stoppage-time strike from Eustáquio condemned South Africa to a 1-0 defeat by Canada in the Round of 32 — a cruel ending to a match they may well feel they deserved more from, edged out only by a goal that arrived when the draw seemed the likeliest outcome. Their tournament is over, then, not with a collapse but with a wound: eliminated in the last breath of normal time, denied a place in the last sixteen by the narrowest and most punishing of margins.

South Korea.text: South Korea's group stage ended with a 0-1 defeat to South Africa, Maseko's 63rd-minute strike enough to send the Taeguk Warriors out on the wrong note — a flat, uninspiring exit that offered little in mitigation. They had scrapped through as one of the best third-placed sides, but the tournament caught up with them in Atlanta, and a side that never quite found cohesion or cutting edge departed at the first hurdle, their World Cup lasting exactly three matches and yielding just three points.

Spain.text: Spain closed out Group H with the same economy of means that has defined their tournament — a Baena strike just before the break enough to see off Uruguay in a controlled, largely comfortable win that left their clean sheet intact across three matches. Austria await in the Round of 32 on Thu Jul 2 12:00 PT, a manageable assignment on paper for the reigning European champions, though the Austrians are no pushover and Spain's measured group-stage performances suggest they have gears still to find. Should they advance, Croatia or Portugal could loom in the Round of 16 — a genuinely combustible proposition — before the draw potentially delivers France or Germany in the semifinals.

Sweden.text: Sweden scraped through the group stage on the slenderest of margins, their 1-1 draw with Japan — Elanga equalising eight minutes after Maeda's opener — a nervy, reactive display that barely flattered a side carrying a seven-goal swing in their goal difference. They advanced as one of the best third-placed teams, which tells its own story about the level of performance so far. The reward is France in the Round of 32 on Tue Jun 30 14:00 PT, a fixture that, on current evidence, constitutes a severe examination: Les Bleus arrive as heavy favourites, and Sweden will need to be considerably more convincing than they were against Japan to harbour any realistic ambition. Survive that, and Germany or Paraguay await in the Round of 16 — with the Dutch, Moroccan, or Canadian sides potentially looming in the quarterfinal beyond.

Switzerland.text: Switzerland topped Group B with a controlled, if not entirely serene, performance against Canada — two goals in the opening eleven minutes of the second half put them in command before Jonathan David pulled one back late to add unnecessary tension. They meet Algeria in the Round of 32 on Thu Jul 2 20:00 PT, a tie that favors the Swiss on paper; Algeria are a dangerous side on their day but Switzerland's solidity and group-stage momentum make them reasonable favorites. Survive that, and Colombia or Ghana await in the Round of 16, with the quarterfinal potentially delivering Argentina as the tournament's sternest examination of Switzerland's ceiling.

Tunisia.text:
There are a few data issues worth flagging before writing this blurb.

The `status` field says Tunisia "advanced as a best third-placed team out of Group F," but `finish` says "4th place," `eliminated` is true, and the record shows 0 points from three losses — a team that cannot have advanced. The `last_match` also has an apparent error: "Skhiri 3'" and "Hecke 62'" appear in `opponent_goals` (Netherlands), but Skhiri and Hecke are not Dutch players — Skhiri is Tunisian, and Hecke is associated with Belgian/Dutch club football but the own-goal or misattribution here is unclear. Most strikingly, one of Tunisia's listed `opponent_goals` is likely a Tunisian own goal or a data-entry mix-up.

I don't want to invent a clean narrative around contradictory facts. Could you clarify:

1. Did Tunisia finish 4th and exit, or did they genuinely advance?
2. Are the goal attributions correct — specifically, were Skhiri and Hecke own goals credited to Netherlands, or are they misplaced in the wrong scorer list?

Once the brief is consistent, I'll write the blurb accurately.

Turkey.text: Turkey's group stage exit arrived with the cruel drama their tournament deserved — a 3-2 win over the United States that was ultimately rendered meaningless in the standings. The Americans struck first through Trusty inside three minutes, yet Güler and Yilmaz turned things around before half-time, only for Berhalter to level early in the second half; Ayhan's stoppage-time header deep into added time sealed the points but not progress. Turkey finished fourth in Group D, their one victory coming too late and too narrow in goal difference to carry them through, and so a tournament of intermittent promise ends without a knockout stage to show for it.

USA.text: The United States closed the group stage with a bruising 2-3 defeat to Turkey — Trusty's third-minute opener promised much, but Turkey turned the game around by the half-hour and then buried any hope of a comeback with a gut-punch in the ninth minute of stoppage time, a result that stings despite the group win. The hosts advance to face Bosnia & Herzegovina in the Round of 32 on Wed Jul 1 17:00 PT, a tie where American expectation will be high on home soil, though Bosnia are capable of disrupting a team whose defensive frailties Turkey already exposed. Survive that, and Belgium or Senegal await in the Round of 16 — and a potential quarterfinal collision with Portugal or Spain would be the true examination of where this American side actually stands.

Uruguay.text: Uruguay's group stage ended with a narrow 0-1 defeat to Spain, Baena's first-half strike proving the solitary difference in a contest that felt tighter than the scoreline suggests — a game Uruguay never quite unlocked despite staying in it until the final whistle. They leave the tournament having advanced as one of the best third-placed teams, only to find no road beyond the group: two draws and a single defeat across three matches tell the story of a side that ground out points without ever imposing themselves, their tournament concluding in the group stage of World Cup 2026.

Uzbekistan.text: Uzbekistan signed off from their maiden World Cup with a 1-3 defeat to DR Congo that told a familiar story: an early Shomurodov strike offered fleeting promise, before the Congolese took control with a penalty from Wissa, a Mayele effort, and a late third that confirmed the margin was entirely deserved. They finish bottom of Group K without a point, having conceded eleven goals across three matches — a brutal education at football's highest level. Central Asia's standard-bearers depart having shown courage simply in qualifying, but the gap in class exposed here gives the next generation a clear-eyed reckoning to build from.
